2: The primary sources of Canada's agricultural greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions are due to the demand for cattle products, mainly from beef and milk. In 2013, an estimated 60 million tonnes (Mt) CO2eq were emitted directly from the agricultural sector of Canada where 59% of these emissions were due to both livestock related enteric fermentation and manure management, and cattle rearing

was responsible for 96% of these livestock GHG emissions (Environment Canada, 2015). While enteric methane emissions accounted for 42% of total emissions from the agricultural sector, 17% (~10.2 Mt CO2eq) were due to manure, indicating that the improved management of manure represents a sizeable GHG mitigation opportunity with no discernable impact on animal productivity (Environment Canada, 2015). In Canada, a recent survey found that 72% of the manure generated from lactating dairy cows was handled as slurry and only about 10% of farms with slurry storage separated solids from liquids (Sheppard et al., 2011). As a result, the majority of dairy slurry in Canada remains untreated and is stored primarily in plastic or

concrete pits (53%), lagoons/dugouts (39%) or in above-ground tanks (10%) (Sheppard et al., 2011). The amount of untreated manure prior to field spreading is reflective of the number of Ca- nadian farms that have environmental farm plans where only 35% of surveyed farms had one, and only 43% of the farms with a plan had fully implemented beneficial management practices (StatCan, 2011). The dairy sector in Canada still has a lot of operating space for adopting best manure management practices that can result in significant reductions in GHG emissions.

stage to another. To date, most dairy cattle life cycle assessments (LCA) and carbon footprint assessments (CFA), both in Canada and internationally, have been conducted using a combination of Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) tier 1 and 2 (IPCC, 2006) manure-based GHG emission factors. Arsenault et al. (2009) considered two LCA case studies in Nova Scotia where both a pasture-confinement (150 days on pasture and 215 days in confinement) and a year-round confinement system were considered. Jayasundara and Wagner-Riddle (2013) under- took a CFA of Ontario's cow-milk production where they used a combination of bottom-up process-based and top down Ontario- wide statistics to determine the necessary inputs and outputs of the system. On the other hand, Mc Geough et al. (2012) undertook a dairy farm CFA in the province of Quebec assuming a confinement tie-stall case study whereas Vergé et al. (2007, 2013) applied a top- down statistical approach to provide province-specific CFA results for dairy farm products across Canada. From a Canadian perspec- tive, Arsenault et al. (2009), Mc Geough et al. (2012), Zhang et al. (2013) and Hagemann et al. (2011) conducted farm specific studies, whereas the works of Vergé et al. (2007, 2013), Jayasundara and Wagner-Riddle (2013) and Quantis et al. (2012) performed regionalized analyses, and when indicated, these studies utilized tier 2 methods for manure GHG emissions. The pattern of using IPCC tier 1 and 2 emission factors is also prevalent from an international dairy LCA perspective. For instance, Pirlo (2012) undertook a dairy LCA review and found that most studies applied the IPCC (2006) tier 1 or 2 methodology; however, a declaration of whether tier 1 or 2 emission factors were employed was rarely stated. Baldini et al. (2016)'s milk LCA review was also in agreement with this general trend where they found a consensus in the estimation of GHG emissions for which IPCC tier 2 equations and related reference values were generally used but often not stated (Notarnicola et al., 2015). This trend is discouraging given that recent studies measuring methane from liquid manure storage 1 Tier 1: A simplified method that only requires livestock population data by animal species/category and climate region in combination with IPCC default emission factors; tier 2: a more complex method than tier 1 that requires detailed information on animal characteristics and manure management practices; tier 3: a more complex approach than tier 2 that utilizes country-specific measurements or modeling approaches (see IPCC, 2006 for details).

A number of recent studies quantified the GHG emissions and reduction opportunities for manure composting systems using both simple and more complex technologies. Results indicate that composting is superior to no treatment because methane emissions with relatively high radiative forcing are replaced with CO2 emis- sions (Fillingham et al., under review; Pattey et al., 2005; Thompson et al., 2004). Pattey et al. (2005) compared three options of dairy and beef manure management: slurry, stockpile, and passively aerated composting. In terms of radiative forcing, they found that the GHG emissions from the slurry storage led to twice and four to seven times as much radiative forcing than the composting option for dairy and beef cattle manure slurry, respectively.

The urgent need to shift towards a sustainable food system has given rise to a widespread increase in the number of food based life cycle assessment (LCA) studies. Applicable to all products and services, LCA was internationally standardised by the ISO 14040 and 14044 standards (ISO, 2006a, b). The primary strength of LCA is its systems perspective which can account for any potential of burden shifting either between type of impact or from one process

Similarly, Thompson et al. (2004) measured emissions from in- vessel composting of liquid swine manure and found that the actively aerated composter reduced N20 and CH4 emissions to as low as 30% in comparison to storage without treatment. Fillingham et al. (under review) measured and compared the GHG emissions between solid-liquid separation followed by in-vessel composting, versus a traditional (unseparated) liquid manure storage (i.e. con- crete tank) system. They found that the total GHG emissions (separated storage) were reduced by up to 79% when compared to the traditional liquid storage method; however, a trade-off was observed where ammonia emissions were emitted at higher rates from the composter than reported values for the traditional storage system.

(VanderZaag et al., 2013; Baldé et al., 2016) have found that the emission factors derived from measurements were significantly higher (1.7-5.1 times larger) than methane emission factors derived from IPCC tier 1 and 2 approaches.

The aforementioned findings indicate a need for more dairy LCA studies that utilize tier 31 approaches in which GHG emission es- timates from mechanistic models parameterized using farm-based measurements are utilized where each stage of the manure man- agement cycle is made explicit. The objective of this research was to undertake a detailed comparative dairy LCA based on a dairy farm located in Ontario, Canada that recently adopted an active com- posting system that incorporated a screw-press solids/liquids separator prior to in-vessel composting (Baldé et al., 2016; Fillingham et al., under review). In achieving this goal, farmers and policy makers can become more informed about the environ- mental trade-offs of utilizing this technology. By undertaking a life- cycle perspective (from cradle to milk at farm gate), all trade-offs between the active manure composting system, and the farm with the conventional liquid slurry storage system can be compared fairly.

Apart from the clear differences in direct GHG emissions from manure, there are other important trade-offs to consider: differ- ences in crop soil emissions and carbon sequestration rates in the local dairy crop complex (DCC); increased electricity demand for the dairy farm employing the composter technology; differing manure spreading technology requirements; and differing bedding materials (sand versus composted manure). Choosing a functional unit that is representative of the primary product of the dairy farm (i.e. milk) allows for a robust quantitative assessment of how the GHG emissions reduction potential of the composting system can reduce the carbon footprint of milk. The selected functional unit for this analysis also permits a straightforward benchmarking pro- cedure with other dairy LCA studies and this has been included in Section 3.5.

Choosing a functional unit that is representative of the primary product of the dairy farm (i.e. milk) allows for a robust quantitative assessment of how the GHG emissions reduction potential of the composting system can reduce the carbon footprint of milk. The selected functional unit for this analysis also permits a straightforward benchmarking pro- cedure with other dairy LCA studies and this has been included in Section 3.5.

stage to another. To date, most dairy cattle life cycle assessments (LCA) and carbon footprint assessments (CFA), both in Canada and internationally, have been conducted using a combination of Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC) tier 1 and 2 (IPCC, 2006) manure-based GHG emission factors. Arsenault et al. (2009) considered two LCA case studies in Nova Scotia where both a pasture-confinement (150 days on pasture and 215 days in confinement) and a year-round confinement system were considered. Jayasundara and Wagner-Riddle (2013) under- took a CFA of Ontario's cow-milk production where they used a combination of bottom-up process-based and top down Ontario- wide statistics to determine the necessary inputs and outputs of the system. On the other hand, Mc Geough et al. (2012) undertook a dairy farm CFA in the province of Quebec assuming a confinement tie-stall case study whereas Vergé et al. (2007, 2013) applied a top- down statistical approach to provide province-specific CFA results for dairy farm products across Canada. From a Canadian perspec- tive, Arsenault et al. (2009), Mc Geough et al. (2012), Zhang et al. (2013) and Hagemann et al. (2011) conducted farm specific studies, whereas the works of Vergé et al. (2007, 2013), Jayasundara and Wagner-Riddle (2013) and Quantis et al. (2012) performed regionalized analyses, and when indicated, these studies utilized tier 2 methods for manure GHG emissions. The pattern of using IPCC tier 1 and 2 emission factors is also prevalent from an international dairy LCA perspective. For instance, Pirlo (2012) undertook a dairy LCA review and found that most studies applied the IPCC (2006) tier 1 or 2 methodology; however, a declaration of whether tier 1 or 2 emission factors were employed was rarely stated. Baldini et al. (2016)'s milk LCA review was also in agreement with this general trend where they found a consensus in the estimation of GHG emissions for which IPCC tier 2 equations and related reference values were generally used but often not stated (Notarnicola et al., 2015). This trend is discouraging given that recent studies measuring methane from liquid manure storage 1 Tier 1: A simplified method that only requires livestock population data by animal species/category and climate region in combination with IPCC default emission factors; tier 2: a more complex method than tier 1 that requires detailed information on animal characteristics and manure management practices; tier 3: a more complex approach than tier 2 that utilizes country-specific measurements or modeling approaches (see IPCC, 2006 for details).

A number of recent studies quantified the GHG emissions and reduction opportunities for manure composting systems using both simple and more complex technologies. Results indicate that composting is superior to no treatment because methane emissions with relatively high radiative forcing are replaced with CO2 emis- sions (Fillingham et al., under review; Pattey et al., 2005; Thompson et al., 2004). Pattey et al. (2005) compared three options of dairy and beef manure management: slurry, stockpile, and passively aerated composting. In terms of radiative forcing, they found that the GHG emissions from the slurry storage led to twice and four to seven times as much radiative forcing than the composting option for dairy and beef cattle manure slurry, respectively.

The urgent need to shift towards a sustainable food system has given rise to a widespread increase in the number of food based life cycle assessment (LCA) studies. Applicable to all products and services, LCA was internationally standardised by the ISO 14040 and 14044 standards (ISO, 2006a, b). The primary strength of LCA is its systems perspective which can account for any potential of burden shifting either between type of impact or from one process

Similarly, Thompson et al. (2004) measured emissions from in- vessel composting of liquid swine manure and found that the actively aerated composter reduced N20 and CH4 emissions to as low as 30% in comparison to storage without treatment. Fillingham et al. (under review) measured and compared the GHG emissions between solid-liquid separation followed by in-vessel composting, versus a traditional (unseparated) liquid manure storage (i.e. con- crete tank) system. They found that the total GHG emissions (separated storage) were reduced by up to 79% when compared to the traditional liquid storage method; however, a trade-off was observed where ammonia emissions were emitted at higher rates from the composter than reported values for the traditional storage system.

(VanderZaag et al., 2013; Baldé et al., 2016) have found that the emission factors derived from measurements were significantly higher (1.7-5.1 times larger) than methane emission factors derived from IPCC tier 1 and 2 approaches.

The aforementioned findings indicate a need for more dairy LCA studies that utilize tier 31 approaches in which GHG emission es- timates from mechanistic models parameterized using farm-based measurements are utilized where each stage of the manure man- agement cycle is made explicit. The objective of this research was to undertake a detailed comparative dairy LCA based on a dairy farm located in Ontario, Canada that recently adopted an active com- posting system that incorporated a screw-press solids/liquids separator prior to in-vessel composting (Baldé et al., 2016; Fillingham et al., under review). In achieving this goal, farmers and policy makers can become more informed about the environ- mental trade-offs of utilizing this technology. By undertaking a life- cycle perspective (from cradle to milk at farm gate), all trade-offs between the active manure composting system, and the farm with the conventional liquid slurry storage system can be compared fairly.

Apart from the clear differences in direct GHG emissions from manure, there are other important trade-offs to consider: differ- ences in crop soil emissions and carbon sequestration rates in the local dairy crop complex (DCC); increased electricity demand for the dairy farm employing the composter technology; differing manure spreading technology requirements; and differing bedding materials (sand versus composted manure). Choosing a functional unit that is representative of the primary product of the dairy farm (i.e. milk) allows for a robust quantitative assessment of how the GHG emissions reduction potential of the composting system can reduce the carbon footprint of milk. The selected functional unit for this analysis also permits a straightforward benchmarking pro- cedure with other dairy LCA studies and this has been included in Section 3.5.

7: <header>2.1. Goal and scope of the study </header>

The goal of this study was to determine the environmental trade-offs of using an automated manure composter-separator technology within a dairy farm system. To achieve this goal, a comparative attributional LCA based on measured data (Jun. 2013-Dec. 2014) from a commercial dairy farm located near Ottawa, Ontario, Canada, was completed. The actual farm utilized an innovative screw-press separator coupled with an active com- poster (i.e. rotating drum) for treating liquid manure (dairy farm with composter [DFC] herein). The DFC system was compared to a dairy farm baseline (DFB herein) system representing a typical dairy farm operation for the region where raw liquid manure was stored directly in an open storage tank before being spread on cropland as an organic fertilizer (i.e. nutrient source and soil amendment). The composted manure in the DFC was used as both a fertilizer and a bedding material for the cattle on site, whereas in the DFB system, imported sand was the assumed bedding material (sand was the bedding material at the farm prior to the inclusion of the compost system). Fig. 1 provides an overview of the cradle-to- farm-gate life cycle system areas (SA) and key processes considered for both the DFC and the DFB systems.

In Fig. 1, the processes/arrows highlighted in blue dots (====) are only included in the DFB system while the processes/arrows highlighted in green dashes (---) are only included in the DFC system. All other processes/arrows (in black) are the same for both dairy systems. One should keep in mind that the process activities common to both systems can still vary in terms of process input

requirements and emission outputs. For instance, while both sys- items require electricity from the Ontario grid mix, the DFC system demanded significantly more electrical energy (1.5 times more) due to the automated separator-composter technology. For process specific input-output details, see the Supplementary Materials (SM) which provides gate-to-gate inventory for each foreground process considered within each SA. Important process exclusions include veterinary medicines, transportation of workers, and a number of background contributions that are not accounted for in the ecoin- vent v2.2 database (ecoinvent v2.2, 2010) which was used as a background system-see Majeau-Bettez et al. (2011) for details.

Following the ReCiPe methodology (ReCiPe, 2016), five midpoint impact categories were considered: global warming potential, freshwater eutrophication potential (FWEP), terrestrial acidifica- tion potential (TAP), photochemical oxidant formation potential (POFP) and fossil fuel depletion potential (FFDP).
8: <header>2.1. Goal and scope of the study </header>

The goal of this study was to determine the environmental trade-offs of using an automated manure composter-separator technology within a dairy farm system. To achieve this goal, a comparative attributional LCA based on measured data (Jun. 2013-Dec. 2014) from a commercial dairy farm located near Ottawa, Ontario, Canada, was completed. The actual farm utilized an innovative screw-press separator coupled with an active com- poster (i.e. rotating drum) for treating liquid manure (dairy farm with composter [DFC] herein). The DFC system was compared to a dairy farm baseline (DFB herein) system representing a typical dairy farm operation for the region where raw liquid manure was stored directly in an open storage tank before being spread on cropland as an organic fertilizer (i.e. nutrient source and soil amendment). The composted manure in the DFC was used as both a fertilizer and a bedding material for the cattle on site, whereas in the DFB system, imported sand was the assumed bedding material (sand was the bedding material at the farm prior to the inclusion of the compost system). Fig. 1 provides an overview of the cradle-to- farm-gate life cycle system areas (SA) and key processes considered for both the DFC and the DFB systems.

In Fig. 1, the processes/arrows highlighted in blue dots (====) are only included in the DFB system while the processes/arrows highlighted in green dashes (---) are only included in the DFC system. All other processes/arrows (in black) are the same for both dairy systems. One should keep in mind that the process activities common to both systems can still vary in terms of process input

requirements and emission outputs. For instance, while both sys- items require electricity from the Ontario grid mix, the DFC system demanded significantly more electrical energy (1.5 times more) due to the automated separator-composter technology. For process specific input-output details, see the Supplementary Materials (SM) which provides gate-to-gate inventory for each foreground process considered within each SA. Important process exclusions include veterinary medicines, transportation of workers, and a number of background contributions that are not accounted for in the ecoin- vent v2.2 database (ecoinvent v2.2, 2010) which was used as a background system-see Majeau-Bettez et al. (2011) for details.

Following the ReCiPe methodology (ReCiPe, 2016), five midpoint impact categories were considered: global warming potential, freshwater eutrophication potential (FWEP), terrestrial acidifica- tion potential (TAP), photochemical oxidant formation potential (POFP) and fossil fuel depletion potential (FFDP).

11: The functional unit was defined as 1 kg of fat and protein cor- rected milk (FPCM) (see SM: tab 'FU_allocation' for more details). The annual raw milk production across the herd was assumed to be 1.91 M kg-yr-1 (1.81 M kg FPCM yr-1) and was based on farm- specific average monthly daily milking rates across the lactating cattle herd (CanWest DHI, 2014). The allocation approach follows IDF (2010)'s default approach-85.6% to milk and 14.4% to meat (for details see SM: tab 'FU_allocation').
12: A steady-state cattle herd structure that is representative of the recorded dairy cattle population was determined using a dairy management tool from the University of Wisconsin (Cabrera and Meyer, 2016). Seven parameters were required to estimate the herd structure and typical Ontario-specific (or nearby regions) values were chosen: i) time of first calving, 26.7 months (Grexton, 2016); ii) the proportion of female calves born, 49% (Mc Geough et al., 2012); iii) the stillbirth rate (die at calving or shortly after), 10% (Murray, 2011); iv) the proportion of calves and heifers culled, 15.3% (Cabrera and Meyer, 2016); v) the cow replacement rate, 37% (Jayasundara and Wagner-Riddle, 2013); vi) the cow calving inter- val, 14.2 months (CanWest 2012); and vii) the number of adult cows (both milking and dry) assumed at steady state, 166 head (Baldé et al., 2016).

The steady-state number of milking cows for the farm was based on the annual average lactating cow population recorded at the farm, which was found to be +/-6 (Baldé et al., 2016). The daily average overall feed quantity and feed composition was based on farmer surveys and measured data (Baldé et al., 2016). The feed input for heifers (>6 months age) and adult cows was distributed across several cattle classes (see Table 1) based on daily dry matter intake requirements as tabularized in the Merck Veterinary Manual (Herdt, 2014) where values stemmed from NRC (2001).

Table 1 provides an overview of the steady-state herd popula- tion, along with cattle class specific diet composition, dietary en- ergy (DE), gross energy intake (GEI), methane conversion factors (Ym) and ash content. For each feed input, the moisture content, DE and ash content were from NRC (2001) while the gross energy content of each feed was taken from several sources (Mader et al., 1974; Sauvant et al., 2004; Tine et al., 2001).

The pellets were produced by a regional feed mill, and after communicating with the farm's nutritionist, a precise composition of the pellets could not be ascertained; however, the general composition consisted mostly of by-products such as soy and canola meal, distiller's dried grains with solubles (DDGS) and
Feed storage in silos, at farm

18: The enteric methane emissions were calculated using a regres- sion model that relates dietary variables to enteric CH4 production

using empirical data from Holstein dairy cows in the northern United States and Canada (see equation 8d of Ellis et al., 2007). The selected model uses the dietary variables dry matter intake, neutral detergent fiber and acid detergent fiber (NRC, 2001). The model was chosen for its low root-mean-square-prediction error and also because Escobar et al. (2013) found that it agreed better than several other enteric CH4 prediction equations evaluated using 172 records of measured CH4 emissions from dairy cows.
19: The manure and discarded bedding generated from the entire cattle herd was assumed to be collected with an automatic scraper twice per day and pumped to an EYS SP-600HD Screw Press Separator with two screens (15 mm and 12.5 mm; Dairtech Inc, Lynden, U.S.A.). Upon separation, the liquid fraction was pumped to an outdoor storage tank, while the solid fraction was sent directly to an in-vessel rotating-drum composter (BeddingMaster 6-32, Daritech Inc.). The rotating composter drum maintained a mini- mum internal temperature of 65.6 °C (150 °F) by a computer- controlled ventilation system and the retention time of the com- posting manure was set to 36 h. The composted manure was then stored in a building to be used for either bedding or fertilizer applied to the local crop complex (see 2.6).

The liquid manure was pumped into an uncovered cylindrical concrete tank for anaerobic liquid storage until it was removed several months later for field application. The liquid manure in the tank was assumed to be emptied twice per year-once before seeding (April 13, 2014) and once after harvest (November 15, 2014) (see Table 2 for application rates and Table 3 for manure charac- teristics and specific emission rates). For the DFB system, the liquid manure storage tank received all of the manure generated from the cattle confined to the barns via the automatic scraper and pump system (i.e. no separation) (Baldé et al., 2016; Fillingham et al., under review).

21: <header>2.6. Dairy crop complex (DCC) </header>

The total mixed ration (TMR) consisted of locally sourced alfalfa and corn grown within two kilometers (km) distance from the main milking barn and the heifer replacement barn. The pellet feed was also mixed in the TMR and fed directly to the milking cows. For the locally produced alfalfa and corn, the soil and growth dynamics were modelled using the Canadian DNDC model (i.e. DeNitrificaiton-DeComposition) (see section 2.8 for model details).

23: Based on farmer input and TMR measurements, an alfalfa (for dry hay)-alfalfa (for haylage)-corn (for grain corn)-corn (for corn silage) rotation was assumed for the local dairy crop complex. In terms of number of cuts per year, the first year of alfalfa was assumed to be harvested twice while the second year alfalfa was harvested three times on an annual basis. Life cycle inventory (LCI) for all farm machinery was accounted for and based on the ecoin- vent v2.2 dataset (ecoinvent, 2010; Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). Where possible, diesel fuel consumption per hectare of farm pro- cess and fuel related air emissions were updated using average diesel consumption values from VCE (2014) which were based on farm survey results from Ontario and several adjacent U.S. states.

Spring cultivating-chiseling tillage (two passes) and autumn moldboard plough (1 pass) were assumed for the corn grain and silage crops. For grain corn, the processes of sowing, combine harvesting, manure spreading (see 2.6.2) and pesticide spraying (see 2.6.3) were also considered. For silage corn, the additional processes of sowing, pesticide spraying, chopping, silage loading, and manure spreading were included. For the dry hay crop, the processes pesticide spraying, manure spreading, haying (by rotary tedder), mowing (by rotary mower), swathing (by rotary wind- rower), baling, and bale loading were accounted for. Lastly, for the haylage crop, the processes pesticide spraying, mowing (by rotary mower), fodder loading, and manure spreading were covered. The local DCC was rain fed only, as this is typical for the region.

25: The nutrients available in the dairy cattle manure (from liquids tank and composter) were adequate for supplying nutrients to the field crops and all of the manure was applied to the local DCC. Two manure applications per year were assumed (Table 2). In the case of the DFC system, the solid manure compost in excess of bedding requirements was sent to the local alfalfa fields, and all manure liquids were applied to the grain and silage corn crops.

In the DFC system, the separated liquids and use of compost for bedding as opposed to sand, allowed for liquid manure spreading using a drag-hose technology because of the lower solids content and lower viscosity of separated liquid manure. For the DFB system, sand bedding was chosen and a tractor-slurry wagon assembly was assumed for manure spreading on cropland. Fuel and several ma- terial inputs for the manure slurry spreading technology were taken from a comparative manure spreading energy analysis in Manitoba, Canada (Wiens et al., 2008). As Table 2 shows, the nu- trients applied to each crop differ with varying degrees of signifi- cance. Despite these differences, the crop yields in the local DCC that were simulated with DNDC were only marginally different between the systems and nearly equal cropland areas were required (see Table 3).

27: Ontario-wide crop-specific statistics were used to estimate the rate of pesticide application on a per hectare basis (OMAFRA, 2010). The pesticides were assumed to be applied once per year for the corn crops and twice and three times per year for the first and second alfalfa crops (i.e. one spray per cut). A regional average diesel fuel consumption factor for pesticide spraying was assumed (VCE, 2014), and capital requirements of the tractor/sprayer were taken from Nemecek and Kagi (2007). To account for the supply chains of the pesticide products, the names of each pesticide used on specific Ontario crops were matched to either exact names or pesticide classes for which a number of cradle-to-gate life cycle inventories exist (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007). The same pesticide applications were assumed for each system.
28: As mentioned in Section 2.3, pellets consisted of a mixture of by- products and mineral supplements assuming same composition as in Adom et al. (2013). On a dry matter basis, the major crop by- product inputs were assumed to be soymeal cake/hulls (60.4%), DDGS (17.6%) and canola meal cake (3.41%). For the soybean and canola crop activities, two U.S. cradle-to-gate LCIs were used as a basis in this study (Nemecek and Kägi, 2007) and adapted to Ontario. These adaptations included changing inputs/outputs by normalizing to the average Ontario soy and canola yields in the year 2014 to 3100 kg/ha and 2400 kg/ha, respectively (StatCan, 2016). Soil N20 emission factors were updated to average Ontario results from a recent study (0.25 g N20/kg wet basis soybean and 0.82 g N20/ kgwet basis canola) (Vergé, database in preparation).

The soy meal/hulls and canola meal are by-products of a seed crushing facility while the DDGS are a by-product of a corn-ethanol production facility. An economic allocation procedure was applied in order to divide the cradle-to-gate environmental impacts of producing these feed by-products (see SM: tab pellet_allocation' for assumptions and specific factors).

30: In this study a comparison between the DFC and the DFB sys- tems was undertaken where gaseous emissions from manure holding tanks, composter and farm structures were modelled using manure-DNDC (Li et al., 2012). Emissions from local crop complexes were simulated using the regionally calibrated DNDCv.CAN model (Smith et al., 2008). The benchmarking of models from previous studies and further calibration of some components of manure- DNDC are described in the following sections.
31: The manure-DNDC model is a biogeochemical model for quan- tifying greenhouse gas and ammonia emissions from livestock manure systems. This model accounts for the interrelationships between environmental factors (such as temperature, moisture, redox potential, pH, substrate concentration gradient etc.), the re- actions (such as decomposition, hydrolysis, ammonia volatilization, de/nitrification, fermentation), and how they influence gaseous (CO2, N20, CH4, NH3) emissions at hourly to daily temporal reso- lution (Li et al., 2012).

In a study by Dutta et al. (manuscript in preparation) the manure- DNDC model was calibrated and validated to simulate NH3 and CH4 emissions from liquid storage tanks in Canada including the con- crete tank at the commercial dairy farm that was used to bench- mark the model. As well, measured emissions data (NH3, CH4, N20 and CO2) from the screw-press composter and stockpiled compost and also enteric CH4 emissions from the dairy barns (Fillingham et al., under review; Baldé et al., 2016) was used in this study to further calibrate rate constants in manure-DNDC such that the observed emissions from the entire farm could be accurately simulated. This model version (as in Li et al., 2012) was then used to estimate emissions for the conceptual DFB and DFC in our study and results were incorporated in the LCI.
32: The manure-DNDC model is a biogeochemical model for quan- tifying greenhouse gas and ammonia emissions from livestock manure systems. This model accounts for the interrelationships between environmental factors (such as temperature, moisture, redox potential, pH, substrate concentration gradient etc.), the re- actions (such as decomposition, hydrolysis, ammonia volatilization, de/nitrification, fermentation), and how they influence gaseous (CO2, N20, CH4, NH3) emissions at hourly to daily temporal reso- lution (Li et al., 2012).

In a study by Dutta et al. (manuscript in preparation) the manure- DNDC model was calibrated and validated to simulate NH3 and CH4 emissions from liquid storage tanks in Canada including the con- crete tank at the commercial dairy farm that was used to bench- mark the model. As well, measured emissions data (NH3, CH4, N20 and CO2) from the screw-press composter and stockpiled compost and also enteric CH4 emissions from the dairy barns (Fillingham et al., under review; Baldé et al., 2016) was used in this study to further calibrate rate constants in manure-DNDC such that the observed emissions from the entire farm could be accurately simulated. This model version (as in Li et al., 2012) was then used to estimate emissions for the conceptual DFB and DFC in our study and results were incorporated in the LCI.
33: The original DNDC model was conceived for field crop systems (Li et al., 1992; Li, 2000) and simulates carbon and nitrogen biochemistry over a wide range of agricultural management, soil and climatic conditions. The model estimates daily crop growth, soil temperature, soil moisture, carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) dy- namics, and trace gas emissions. The Canadian version of DNDC (DNDC v.CAN) has been developed, and tested for its ability to
predict soil temperature, soil water content, soil N content, and N20 emissions at various experimental sites in Canada (Smith et al., 2008).

A 1981-2010 daily climate normal observed at the Ottawa airport (Environment Canada, 2016) was selected to represent the climate for the conceptual farms DFB and DFC in this study. For the crop area of the local DCC, a clay loam soil was assumed since it is representative of the entirety of the local DCC soil area (Hills et al., 1944). The cropping area necessary to meet the feed requirements for the farm was based on regional yield averages. Nutrient uptake requirements for each crop type were scaled to the necessary production levels (OMAFRA, 2016). Alfalfa (dry hay and haylage) was designated to receive all surplus manure that was not required to meet the corn nitrogen demand. In the case of the DFC system, the surplus compost that was not used for bedding was applied entirely to the alfalfa with the corn receiving all available slurry manure. DNDC field crop simulations were constructed using the derived fertilizer management inputs and simulated for each crop type over the entire climate normal with outputs for average annual GHG emissions and N losses passed into the LCI. Table 3 provides key characteristics and emission factors for the local DCC (for crop specific manure applications see SM: tab 'SA5_crop_complex_crops').

The crop and manure emission factors in Table 3 were based on annual DNDC simulation results normalized to the given crop yield or manure output, and some key differences between the systems can be noted. For instance, there is significantly more organic matter more) being removed from the barns in the DFC system than the DFB system due to the inclusion of composted manure as bedding. Change in soil carbon (dSOC) in the local DCC also differs considerably between the two systems as a direct consequence of manure application (see Table 2). The solid manure application to the alfalfa crops in the DFC system leads to higher soil carbon sequestration rates, because overall, 1.5 times more carbon was spread on the local DCC system when compared to the DFB system. Also, crop N20 emissions from the Alfalfa crops were higher for the DFB system whereas the opposite was true to a smaller degree for the corn crops.

36: An uncertainty analysis following ecoinvent's recommended methodology which involves the Pedigree scoring technique was undertaken (Weidema et al., 2013). Using a sample size of 10,000, Latin Hypercube sampling (Helton and Davis, 2003) was performed with the assumption that all non-zero parameters had a lognormal distribution-a distribution that is predominately used in the ecoinvent database (for rationale, see Weidema et al., 2013). The uncertainty analysis approach is further explained in the SM (tab 'uncertainty approach') along with all pedigree scores, the related squares of geometric standard deviations (SM: tab 'uncertainty_- factors'), and the uncertainty results (SM: tab "uncertainty_results")
37: The total climate change impact of the DFB and DFC systems amounted to 1.92 and 1.23 kg CO2eq per kg FPCM at the dairy farm gate, respectively. The manure separator-composter system gen- erates 35.6% less GHG emissions than the DFB system. Fig. 2 illus- trates these overall results using a process contribution breakdown with GHG emissions attributed to these key activities and their life cycle inputs across the supply chain.
The majority of the climate change impact occurs directly within

the boundaries of SA7 (Fig. 1, dairy farm), with 79.8% for the DFB system and 74.0% for the DFC system. In both systems, the greatest hotspot (i.e. direct emission source) is due to storing manure in the concrete storage tank where 55.6% and 34.1% of the total climate change impact of the DFB and DFC system occurs. Methane emis- sions contribute 87.8% (DFB system) and 80.5% (DFC system) to the climate change impacts stemming directly from manure slurry storage tanks. The enteric methane emissions are the next largest source of climate change impact in the life cycle with contributions of 19.7% and 30.7% of the total impacts for the DFB and DFC, respectively.

The life cycle GHG emissions generated at the crop farm gate for the entire DCC becomes an important contribution when aggre- gated together-16.7 and 20.0% for the DFB and DFC systems, respectively. For the DFB system, the greatest four hotspots in the local DCC system occur directly from soil N20 emissions and in the following order: high moisture corn grain (3.8%); corn silage (2.6%); haylage (2.6%); and dry hay (1.3%). On the other hand, in the DFC system, while soil N20 emissions from the high moisture corn (5.6%) and the corn silage (4.0%) are significant points of concern, N20 emissions from the haylage (0.082%) and the dry hay (0.61%) crops contribute little to the overall impact. The main reason for this difference between the systems is that manure slurry (greater reactive N) was applied to the alfalfa crops in the DFB system, whereas in the DFC system, the more stable solid composted manure was applied instead.

The manure composter assembly in the DFC system resulted in 1.49 times more electricity consumption (at power plant gate) than the DFB system. However, the contribution of electricity con- sumption to the overall climate change impacts of producing milk was relatively small: 0.53% for the DFB system and 1.3% for the DFC system. This is largely linked to the Ontario grid mix which currently consists of mostly nuclear (62%) and hydro (24%) power, which have low carbon footprints (IESCO, 2016). It should be noted that GHG emissions due to electricity requirements would contribute more in regions with lots of coal power. For instance, if the grid was 100% coal power, and taking a life cycle emission factor of 1.0 kg KWh- (Singh et al., 2011), the carbon footprint of the DFB and DFC systems would increase to 1.40 and 2.03 kg CO2eq/kg FPCM, respectively. Even in this unlikely scenario, the DFC system would still create a 31% reduction in life-cycle GHG emissions.

Given the short manure transport distances of less than 2 km, the difference between manure spreading technologies leads to marginal effects on the overall life cycle GHG emission results. For the DFC system, manure slurry spreading via drag-hose and solid manure spreading via wagon lead to a combined 6.8 g CO2eq/kg FPCM, while the manure slurry spreading via slurry tanker for the DFB system created 7.4 CO2eq/kg FPCM, respectively. While the DFC system generated less GHG emissions from the diesel com- bustion and capital requirements required to spread the manure, the benefits are minor overall: contributing 0.39% for the DFB system and 0.59% for the DFC system.
38: The total climate change impact of the DFB and DFC systems amounted to 1.92 and 1.23 kg CO2eq per kg FPCM at the dairy farm gate, respectively. The manure separator-composter system gen- erates 35.6% less GHG emissions than the DFB system. Fig. 2 illus- trates these overall results using a process contribution breakdown with GHG emissions attributed to these key activities and their life cycle inputs across the supply chain.

the boundaries of SA7 (Fig. 1, dairy farm), with 79.8% for the DFB system and 74.0% for the DFC system. The enteric methane emissions are the next largest source of climate change impact in the life cycle with contributions of 19.7% and 30.7% of the total impacts for the DFB and DFC, respectively.

42: A structural path analysis (Defourny and Thorbecke, 1984) provides a clear understanding of the climate change hotspots and their specific pathways in the DFB and DFC system. Fig. 3 illustrates the top ten pathways contributing to the overall climate change impact along with the aggregated impact of the remaining path- ways in the system (labelled other pathways). Each node in Fig. 3 represents a process activity, and the nodes denoted with a "+xx %" label indicate how the specific activity directly contributes to the life cycle climate change impacts of producing a kg of FPCM at dairy farm gate.

SA|Process contribution|DFB|DFC 1|Electricity|0.53%|1.3% 2|Truck transport|1.1%|1.8% 2|Train transport|0.018%|0.028% 3|Inorganic fertilizer production|0.23%|0.35% 4|Pesticides production|0.055%|0.084% 5|Corn, high moisture, soil|3.8%|5.6% 5|Corn silage, soil|2.6%|4.0% 5|Dry hay, soil|1.3%|0.61% 5|Haylage, soil|2.6%|0.082% 5|Farm machinery inputs|2.0%|2.8% 6|Pellet production|4.1%|6.4% 6|TMR processing|1.9%|2.9% 7|Dairy farm capital|1.7%|2.3% 7|Manure-barn|1.0%|3.4% 7|Enteric barn|20%|31% 7|Manure composter|N/A|0.25% 7|Manure compost storage|N/A|0.50% 7|Manure Slurry storage|56%|34% 5/7 Indirect N2O, all sources|5/7 Indirect N2O, all sources|1.9%|2.8% total|total|100%|100%
Life cycle GHG emissions (kg CO2eq per F.U.)

Fig. 2. A life cycle process contribution breakdown of the total greenhouse gas emissions for the two dairy systems compared: SA - system area; DFB - dairy farm baseline system; DFC - dairy farm composter system; TMR = total mixed ration.
5-4: Maize seed, at farm 5-5: Haylage production
6-2: Corn storage in silo 6-3: Corn silage storage in silo 6-4: TMR processing
6-10: Natural gas, in furnace 6-11: Haylage storage in silo

Fig. 3. A structural path Sankey diagram of the top ten and remaining pathways contributing to climate change potential (i.e. kg CO2eq kg 1 FPCM, FU = functional unit) in the life cycle system of the DFB - dairy farm baseline system (right) - and the DFC - dairy farm composter system (left). Nodes with "+xx %" are the processes where GHG emissions occur directly.

A total of 70.1% and 77.5% of the life cycle GHG emissions from the DFC and DFB systems occur directly from manure and enteric CH4 emissions in the barn and liquids storage tank. The N20 emissions from crop soil are the next most important hot spot in the system. Even though the life cycle impact of pellet production is significantly larger than all other feed inputs as shown in Fig. 2, there is no single major hotspot of concern. For instance, as Fig. 3 illustrates, the most important hotspots for pellet production are the natural gas required at the distillery where DDGS production occurs (1.6% for DFC and 1.3% for DFB of total), and the transport requirements for the feed inputs to the pellet processing plant (0.30% [DFB system] and 0.47% [DFC system] of total).

There are numerous other pathways in the DFB and DFC system, where each of these pathways contributes less than 0.18% (for DFB) and 0.30% (for DFC) to the total impact per FU while aggregating to a significant 9.03% and 15.3% of the total life cycle climate change impact, respectively. When placing a structural pathway tolerance that accounts for 99.9999% of the total climate change impact in each system, this high tolerance accounts for 11,105 (84% of impact attributed to other pathways) and 15,533 (87% of impact attributed to other pathways) pathways fitting into this other pathways cate- gory for the DFB and DFC system. This indicates there were many pathways that create small GHG contributions, and when
aggregated together, make a significant contribution.

Life cycle GHG emissions (kg CO2eq per F.U.)

Fig. 2. A life cycle process contribution breakdown of the total greenhouse gas emissions for the two dairy systems compared: SA - system area; DFB - dairy farm baseline system; DFC - dairy farm composter system; TMR = total mixed ration.
5-4: Maize seed, at farm 5-5: Haylage production
6-2: Corn storage in silo 6-3: Corn silage storage in silo 6-4: TMR processing
6-10: Natural gas, in furnace 6-11: Haylage storage in silo

Fig. 3. A structural path Sankey diagram of the top ten and remaining pathways contributing to climate change potential (i.e. kg CO2eq kg 1 FPCM, FU = functional unit) in the life cycle system of the DFB - dairy farm baseline system (right) - and the DFC - dairy farm composter system (left). Nodes with "+xx %" are the processes where GHG emissions occur directly.

A total of 70.1% and 77.5% of the life cycle GHG emissions from the DFC and DFB systems occur directly from manure and enteric CH4 emissions in the barn and liquids storage tank. The N20 emissions from crop soil are the next most important hot spot in the system. Even though the life cycle impact of pellet production is significantly larger than all other feed inputs as shown in Fig. 2, there is no single major hotspot of concern. For instance, as Fig. 3 illustrates, the most important hotspots for pellet production are the natural gas required at the distillery where DDGS production occurs (1.6% for DFC and 1.3% for DFB of total), and the transport requirements for the feed inputs to the pellet processing plant (0.30% [DFB system] and 0.47% [DFC system] of total).

There are numerous other pathways in the DFB and DFC system, where each of these pathways contributes less than 0.18% (for DFB) and 0.30% (for DFC) to the total impact per FU while aggregating to a significant 9.03% and 15.3% of the total life cycle climate change impact, respectively. When placing a structural pathway tolerance that accounts for 99.9999% of the total climate change impact in each system, this high tolerance accounts for 11,105 (84% of impact attributed to other pathways) and 15,533 (87% of impact attributed to other pathways) pathways fitting into this other pathways cate- gory for the DFB and DFC system. This indicates there were many pathways that create small GHG contributions, and when
aggregated together, make a significant contribution.

6: The primary focus of this comparative LCA was to illustrate the difference in GHG emissions between two manure management (MM) scenarios. Fig. 4 provides a comparison of how each MM pool contributes to GHG emissions due to the production of a kg of FPCM at farm-gate.

In the barn of the DFC system, the manure-related GWP contribution is 1.58 times higher than in the barn of the DFB sys- tem. The main reason for this difference is because the DFC system utilizes the organic carbon rich manure compost for bedding while the DFB system relies on the inorganic sand bedding material. In both cases, methane emissions contribute to most of the GWP due to the barn manure/bedding pool while NH3 emissions significantly contribute to indirect emissions.

The most important GHG-related difference between the MM systems is the CH4 emissions from the liquid manure storage tank, where the constant anaerobic conditions allow methanogens and other anaerobic bacteria to thrive. Methane emissions from the DFC storage tank were 37% of the CH4 emissions from the DFB storage tank. Since the DFC system employs a screw-press separator,

around 70% of the carbon in the raw manure/bedding is delivered to the active composter and thus creating a significant reduction in volatile solids load in the liquids storage tank.

Soil emissions from the local DCC are the second most important GWP contributor in the MM system where the DFC system per- forms better than the DFB system. In the DFC system, the field application of the composted solid manure to the alfalfa crops plays a two-fold advantage since both N20 emissions are reduced while the rate of soil organic carbon additions are higher than the DFB system.

In total, the GWP due to direct emissions from the MM pools (including soils) in the DFC system is 49.6% of what occurred in the DFB system and this includes indirect N20 emissions from ammonia and nitrate emissions, which were negligible overall. Total GHG and NH3 emissions from manure at daily resolution are provided in the SM (tab: manure_daily_GHGs).

10: The life cycle impact results for the other impact categories suggest only a marginal problem shifting concern for the DFC sys- tem. For the FWEP and FFDP categories, the per FU impacts of the DFC system were 2.2% and 2.4% higher than the DFB system. On the other hand, the DFC system outperformed the DFB system across the TAP and FFDP impact categories with potential reductions of 11%. Detailed process contribution results for these four impact categories is provided in the SM (tab 'other_impact_results').
11: Fig. 5 provides a comparison of the life cycle GHG emissions per kg FPCM (both before and after allocation) between several past Canadian studies and the scenarios in this study using both the tier 3 results and the tier 2 MM CH4 emission factors (see SM: tab "tier2_assumptions" for details). The LCA results from this study are higher than results using the tier 2 CH4 factors along with findings from past studies. The main reason for these differences is due to the assumed GHG emissions from MM (i.e. at dairy farm before field application).
Comparing results before allocation, the GHG emissions gener- ated from MM for the DFB and DFC scenarios (using process

simulations) range from 3.8 to 14 and from 1.7 to 6.1 times higher than these previous studies, respectively. For Arsenault et al. (2009)'s confined farm system, all manure was stored as liquid slurry in a tank and they followed IPCC (1996) tier 2 guidelines which recommends a methane conversion factor (MCF) of 10% (IPCC, 1996). On the other hand, Jayasundara and Wagner-Riddle (2013) apply a tier 2 (IPCC, 2006) weighted average MCF for manure storage where they assumed 40% of manure treatment in the Ontario system was handled as liquids (MCF = 18%), and 43% and 17% of manure was handled as solids (MCF = 2%) or excreted onto pasture (MCF = 1%), respectively. The lower GHG intensity (0.92 kg CO2eq kg FPCM, before allocation) for milk production found by Mc Geough et al. (2012) was mostly explained by the following assumptions: i) the use of a solid manure management system (i.e. MCF = 2%); and ii) CO2eq credits generated from exported manure were used for replacing synthetic fertilizer N in a receiving farm beyond the dairy farm system boundaries. These differences in GHG intensity are observed for similar reasons in a Canada-wide study that estimated the average GHG intensity of raw milk at farm gate to be 1.07 kg CO2eq kg™ milk (inter-pro- vincial averages ranged from 0.86 to 1.14 kg CO2eq kg- milk) (Vergé et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. 5, when the appropriate tier 2 MM CH4 factors are applied in our study (MCF = 17%), then results fall within the range of previous Canadian studies.

For the DFB and DFC systems in this study, the MCFs derived from the average methane rate as determined from the manure- DNDC simulations (tier 3 results) for manure storage in the con- crete tank equalled 66% and 71%, respectively. These estimated MCFs are much higher than the IPPC tier 2 recommendations for concrete storage tanks with natural crust cover (17%) and within the same magnitude as the IPCC tier 2 recommendations for an uncovered anaerobic lagoon with an average annual temperature less than 10° C (MCF = 66%)-the annual average temperature in the region (Ottawa) is about 6° C.

The high manure methane emissions simulated with manure- DNDC for the liquid storage tank when compared to the IPCC rec- ommended factors has been recognized in past studies. For instance, VanderZaag et al. (2013) found that the range of tier 1 (cool climate) methane EFs for liquid dairy manure (132-159 g head d-¹ to be much smaller than average summer and spring factors based on emission measurements: 249 g CH4 cow 1 in

Fig. 5. Carbon footprint contribution analysis per kg FPCM comparison between this study and several previous Canadian studies. Results are presented with and without milk product allocation (see allocation factors, a, of specific studies in figure). Acronymns: NS = Nova Scotia; QC : Quebec; ON = Ontario.

the spring and 673 g CH4 cow in the summer (i.e. ranging from 1.9 to 5.1 times higher). Baldé et al. (2016) also found the U.S. EPA's manure methane emissions model that the IPCC tier 2 MCFs are partly based on (Mangino et al., 2001), tend to underestimate GHG emissions by up to 60% of the total annual observed CH4 emissions from a storage tank.

One challenge when applying the IPCC tier 2 MCFs for liquid manure storage is that one needs to choose between a liquid slurry tank and an anaerobic lagoon. In both cases manure decomposes anaerobically, however, the prescribed MCFs for the tank and lagoon systems are substantially lower for the tank system in cool- to-temperate climates. As a result, estimated liquid manure methane emissions can be underestimated for systems that are defined as liquid slurry storage tanks and not earthen lagoons in cases where manure tanks are not completely emptied as this can enable both systems to achieve similar volatile solid to methane conversion rates (Baldé et al., 2016). For instance, the assumption that 4% of slurry remained in the tank after each emptying event was made and this was typically observed at the actual farm. While the liquid manure storage system in this study is a concrete liquid manure storage tank, the estimated MCFs are closer to the tier 2 MCFs for an anaerobic lagoon system. The simulated MFCs found in this analysis are also in agreement with MCFs based on measure- ments for the same farm system-57% in year 1 and 72% in year 2 (Baldé et al., 2016).
12: Fig. 5 provides a comparison of the life cycle GHG emissions per kg FPCM (both before and after allocation) between several past Canadian studies and the scenarios in this study using both the tier 3 results and the tier 2 MM CH4 emission factors (see SM: tab "tier2_assumptions" for details). The LCA results from this study are higher than results using the tier 2 CH4 factors along with findings from past studies. The main reason for these differences is due to the assumed GHG emissions from MM (i.e. at dairy farm before field application).
Comparing results before allocation, the GHG emissions gener- ated from MM for the DFB and DFC scenarios (using process

simulations) range from 3.8 to 14 and from 1.7 to 6.1 times higher than these previous studies, respectively. For Arsenault et al. (2009)'s confined farm system, all manure was stored as liquid slurry in a tank and they followed IPCC (1996) tier 2 guidelines which recommends a methane conversion factor (MCF) of 10% (IPCC, 1996). On the other hand, Jayasundara and Wagner-Riddle (2013) apply a tier 2 (IPCC, 2006) weighted average MCF for manure storage where they assumed 40% of manure treatment in the Ontario system was handled as liquids (MCF = 18%), and 43% and 17% of manure was handled as solids (MCF = 2%) or excreted onto pasture (MCF = 1%), respectively. The lower GHG intensity (0.92 kg CO2eq kg FPCM, before allocation) for milk production found by Mc Geough et al. (2012) was mostly explained by the following assumptions: i) the use of a solid manure management system (i.e. MCF = 2%); and ii) CO2eq credits generated from exported manure were used for replacing synthetic fertilizer N in a receiving farm beyond the dairy farm system boundaries. These differences in GHG intensity are observed for similar reasons in a Canada-wide study that estimated the average GHG intensity of raw milk at farm gate to be 1.07 kg CO2eq kg™ milk (inter-pro- vincial averages ranged from 0.86 to 1.14 kg CO2eq kg- milk) (Vergé et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. 5, when the appropriate tier 2 MM CH4 factors are applied in our study (MCF = 17%), then results fall within the range of previous Canadian studies.

For the DFB and DFC systems in this study, the MCFs derived from the average methane rate as determined from the manure- DNDC simulations (tier 3 results) for manure storage in the con- crete tank equalled 66% and 71%, respectively. These estimated MCFs are much higher than the IPPC tier 2 recommendations for concrete storage tanks with natural crust cover (17%) and within the same magnitude as the IPCC tier 2 recommendations for an uncovered anaerobic lagoon with an average annual temperature less than 10° C (MCF = 66%)-the annual average temperature in the region (Ottawa) is about 6° C.

The high manure methane emissions simulated with manure- DNDC for the liquid storage tank when compared to the IPCC rec- ommended factors has been recognized in past studies. For instance, VanderZaag et al. (2013) found that the range of tier 1 (cool climate) methane EFs for liquid dairy manure (132-159 g head d-¹ to be much smaller than average summer and spring factors based on emission measurements: 249 g CH4 cow 1 in

14: Fig. 5 provides a comparison of the life cycle GHG emissions per kg FPCM (both before and after allocation) between several past Canadian studies and the scenarios in this study using both the tier 3 results and the tier 2 MM CH4 emission factors (see SM: tab "tier2_assumptions" for details). The LCA results from this study are higher than results using the tier 2 CH4 factors along with findings from past studies. The main reason for these differences is due to the assumed GHG emissions from MM (i.e. at dairy farm before field application).
Comparing results before allocation, the GHG emissions gener- ated from MM for the DFB and DFC scenarios (using process

simulations) range from 3.8 to 14 and from 1.7 to 6.1 times higher than these previous studies, respectively. For Arsenault et al. (2009)'s confined farm system, all manure was stored as liquid slurry in a tank and they followed IPCC (1996) tier 2 guidelines which recommends a methane conversion factor (MCF) of 10% (IPCC, 1996). On the other hand, Jayasundara and Wagner-Riddle (2013) apply a tier 2 (IPCC, 2006) weighted average MCF for manure storage where they assumed 40% of manure treatment in the Ontario system was handled as liquids (MCF = 18%), and 43% and 17% of manure was handled as solids (MCF = 2%) or excreted onto pasture (MCF = 1%), respectively. The lower GHG intensity (0.92 kg CO2eq kg FPCM, before allocation) for milk production found by Mc Geough et al. (2012) was mostly explained by the following assumptions: i) the use of a solid manure management system (i.e. MCF = 2%); and ii) CO2eq credits generated from exported manure were used for replacing synthetic fertilizer N in a receiving farm beyond the dairy farm system boundaries. These differences in GHG intensity are observed for similar reasons in a Canada-wide study that estimated the average GHG intensity of raw milk at farm gate to be 1.07 kg CO2eq kg™ milk (inter-pro- vincial averages ranged from 0.86 to 1.14 kg CO2eq kg- milk) (Vergé et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. 5, when the appropriate tier 2 MM CH4 factors are applied in our study (MCF = 17%), then results fall within the range of previous Canadian studies.

For the DFB and DFC systems in this study, the MCFs derived from the average methane rate as determined from the manure- DNDC simulations (tier 3 results) for manure storage in the con- crete tank equalled 66% and 71%, respectively. These estimated MCFs are much higher than the IPPC tier 2 recommendations for concrete storage tanks with natural crust cover (17%) and within the same magnitude as the IPCC tier 2 recommendations for an uncovered anaerobic lagoon with an average annual temperature less than 10° C (MCF = 66%)-the annual average temperature in the region (Ottawa) is about 6° C.

The high manure methane emissions simulated with manure- DNDC for the liquid storage tank when compared to the IPCC rec- ommended factors has been recognized in past studies. For instance, VanderZaag et al. (2013) found that the range of tier 1 (cool climate) methane EFs for liquid dairy manure (132-159 g head d-¹ to be much smaller than average summer and spring factors based on emission measurements: 249 g CH4 cow 1 in

(2016) also found the U.S. EPA's manure methane emissions model that the IPCC tier 2 MCFs are partly based on (Mangino et al., 2001), tend to underestimate GHG emissions by up to 60% of the total annual observed CH4 emissions from a storage tank.

One challenge when applying the IPCC tier 2 MCFs for liquid manure storage is that one needs to choose between a liquid slurry tank and an anaerobic lagoon. In both cases manure decomposes anaerobically, however, the prescribed MCFs for the tank and lagoon systems are substantially lower for the tank system in cool- to-temperate climates. As a result, estimated liquid manure methane emissions can be underestimated for systems that are defined as liquid slurry storage tanks and not earthen lagoons in cases where manure tanks are not completely emptied as this can enable both systems to achieve similar volatile solid to methane conversion rates (Baldé et al., 2016). For instance, the assumption that 4% of slurry remained in the tank after each emptying event was made and this was typically observed at the actual farm. While the liquid manure storage system in this study is a concrete liquid manure storage tank, the estimated MCFs are closer to the tier 2 MCFs for an anaerobic lagoon system. The simulated MFCs found in this analysis are also in agreement with MCFs based on measure- ments for the same farm system-57% in year 1 and 72% in year 2 (Baldé et al., 2016).

Results are presented with and without milk product allocation (see allocation factors, a, of specific studies in figure). Acronymns: NS = Nova Scotia; QC : Quebec; ON = Ontario.

the spring and 673 g CH4 cow in the summer (i.e. ranging from 1.9 to 5.1 times higher). Baldé et al. (2016) also found the U.S. EPA's manure methane emissions model that the IPCC tier 2 MCFs are partly based on (Mangino et al., 2001), tend to underestimate GHG emissions by up to 60% of the total annual observed CH4 emissions from a storage tank.

One challenge when applying the IPCC tier 2 MCFs for liquid manure storage is that one needs to choose between a liquid slurry tank and an anaerobic lagoon. In both cases manure decomposes anaerobically, however, the prescribed MCFs for the tank and lagoon systems are substantially lower for the tank system in cool- to-temperate climates. As a result, estimated liquid manure methane emissions can be underestimated for systems that are defined as liquid slurry storage tanks and not earthen lagoons in cases where manure tanks are not completely emptied as this can enable both systems to achieve similar volatile solid to methane conversion rates (Baldé et al., 2016). For instance, the assumption that 4% of slurry remained in the tank after each emptying event was made and this was typically observed at the actual farm. While the liquid manure storage system in this study is a concrete liquid manure storage tank, the estimated MCFs are closer to the tier 2 MCFs for an anaerobic lagoon system. The simulated MFCs found in this analysis are also in agreement with MCFs based on measure- ments for the same farm system-57% in year 1 and 72% in year 2 (Baldé et al., 2016).
17: Fig. 5 provides a comparison of the life cycle GHG emissions per kg FPCM (both before and after allocation) between several past Canadian studies and the scenarios in this study using both the tier 3 results and the tier 2 MM CH4 emission factors (see SM: tab "tier2_assumptions" for details). The LCA results from this study are higher than results using the tier 2 CH4 factors along with findings from past studies. The main reason for these differences is due to the assumed GHG emissions from MM (i.e. at dairy farm before field application).
Comparing results before allocation, the GHG emissions gener- ated from MM for the DFB and DFC scenarios (using process

simulations) range from 3.8 to 14 and from 1.7 to 6.1 times higher than these previous studies, respectively. For Arsenault et al. (2009)'s confined farm system, all manure was stored as liquid slurry in a tank and they followed IPCC (1996) tier 2 guidelines which recommends a methane conversion factor (MCF) of 10% (IPCC, 1996). On the other hand, Jayasundara and Wagner-Riddle (2013) apply a tier 2 (IPCC, 2006) weighted average MCF for manure storage where they assumed 40% of manure treatment in the Ontario system was handled as liquids (MCF = 18%), and 43% and 17% of manure was handled as solids (MCF = 2%) or excreted onto pasture (MCF = 1%), respectively. The lower GHG intensity (0.92 kg CO2eq kg FPCM, before allocation) for milk production found by Mc Geough et al. (2012) was mostly explained by the following assumptions: i) the use of a solid manure management system (i.e. MCF = 2%); and ii) CO2eq credits generated from exported manure were used for replacing synthetic fertilizer N in a receiving farm beyond the dairy farm system boundaries. These differences in GHG intensity are observed for similar reasons in a Canada-wide study that estimated the average GHG intensity of raw milk at farm gate to be 1.07 kg CO2eq kg™ milk (inter-pro- vincial averages ranged from 0.86 to 1.14 kg CO2eq kg- milk) (Vergé et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. 5, when the appropriate tier 2 MM CH4 factors are applied in our study (MCF = 17%), then results fall within the range of previous Canadian studies.

For the DFB and DFC systems in this study, the MCFs derived from the average methane rate as determined from the manure- DNDC simulations (tier 3 results) for manure storage in the con- crete tank equalled 66% and 71%, respectively. These estimated MCFs are much higher than the IPPC tier 2 recommendations for concrete storage tanks with natural crust cover (17%) and within the same magnitude as the IPCC tier 2 recommendations for an uncovered anaerobic lagoon with an average annual temperature less than 10° C (MCF = 66%)-the annual average temperature in the region (Ottawa) is about 6° C.

The high manure methane emissions simulated with manure- DNDC for the liquid storage tank when compared to the IPCC rec- ommended factors has been recognized in past studies. For instance, VanderZaag et al. (2013) found that the range of tier 1 (cool climate) methane EFs for liquid dairy manure (132-159 g head d-¹ to be much smaller than average summer and spring factors based on emission measurements: 249 g CH4 cow 1 in

Fig. 5. Carbon footprint contribution analysis per kg FPCM comparison between this study and several previous Canadian studies. Results are presented with and without milk product allocation (see allocation factors, a, of specific studies in figure). Acronymns: NS = Nova Scotia; QC : Quebec; ON = Ontario.

the spring and 673 g CH4 cow in the summer (i.e. ranging from 1.9 to 5.1 times higher). Baldé et al. (2016) also found the U.S. EPA's manure methane emissions model that the IPCC tier 2 MCFs are partly based on (Mangino et al., 2001), tend to underestimate GHG emissions by up to 60% of the total annual observed CH4 emissions from a storage tank.

One challenge when applying the IPCC tier 2 MCFs for liquid manure storage is that one needs to choose between a liquid slurry tank and an anaerobic lagoon. In both cases manure decomposes anaerobically, however, the prescribed MCFs for the tank and lagoon systems are substantially lower for the tank system in cool- to-temperate climates. As a result, estimated liquid manure methane emissions can be underestimated for systems that are defined as liquid slurry storage tanks and not earthen lagoons in cases where manure tanks are not completely emptied as this can enable both systems to achieve similar volatile solid to methane conversion rates (Baldé et al., 2016). For instance, the assumption that 4% of slurry remained in the tank after each emptying event was made and this was typically observed at the actual farm. While the liquid manure storage system in this study is a concrete liquid manure storage tank, the estimated MCFs are closer to the tier 2 MCFs for an anaerobic lagoon system. The simulated MFCs found in this analysis are also in agreement with MCFs based on measure- ments for the same farm system-57% in year 1 and 72% in year 2 (Baldé et al., 2016).
19: Fig. 5 provides a comparison of the life cycle GHG emissions per kg FPCM (both before and after allocation) between several past Canadian studies and the scenarios in this study using both the tier 3 results and the tier 2 MM CH4 emission factors (see SM: tab "tier2_assumptions" for details). The LCA results from this study are higher than results using the tier 2 CH4 factors along with findings from past studies. The main reason for these differences is due to the assumed GHG emissions from MM (i.e. at dairy farm before field application).
Comparing results before allocation, the GHG emissions gener- ated from MM for the DFB and DFC scenarios (using process

simulations) range from 3.8 to 14 and from 1.7 to 6.1 times higher than these previous studies, respectively. For Arsenault et al. (2009)'s confined farm system, all manure was stored as liquid slurry in a tank and they followed IPCC (1996) tier 2 guidelines which recommends a methane conversion factor (MCF) of 10% (IPCC, 1996). On the other hand, Jayasundara and Wagner-Riddle (2013) apply a tier 2 (IPCC, 2006) weighted average MCF for manure storage where they assumed 40% of manure treatment in the Ontario system was handled as liquids (MCF = 18%), and 43% and 17% of manure was handled as solids (MCF = 2%) or excreted onto pasture (MCF = 1%), respectively. The lower GHG intensity (0.92 kg CO2eq kg FPCM, before allocation) for milk production found by Mc Geough et al. (2012) was mostly explained by the following assumptions: i) the use of a solid manure management system (i.e. MCF = 2%); and ii) CO2eq credits generated from exported manure were used for replacing synthetic fertilizer N in a receiving farm beyond the dairy farm system boundaries. These differences in GHG intensity are observed for similar reasons in a Canada-wide study that estimated the average GHG intensity of raw milk at farm gate to be 1.07 kg CO2eq kg™ milk (inter-pro- vincial averages ranged from 0.86 to 1.14 kg CO2eq kg- milk) (Vergé et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. 5, when the appropriate tier 2 MM CH4 factors are applied in our study (MCF = 17%), then results fall within the range of previous Canadian studies.

For the DFB and DFC systems in this study, the MCFs derived from the average methane rate as determined from the manure- DNDC simulations (tier 3 results) for manure storage in the con- crete tank equalled 66% and 71%, respectively. These estimated MCFs are much higher than the IPPC tier 2 recommendations for concrete storage tanks with natural crust cover (17%) and within the same magnitude as the IPCC tier 2 recommendations for an uncovered anaerobic lagoon with an average annual temperature less than 10° C (MCF = 66%)-the annual average temperature in the region (Ottawa) is about 6° C.

The high manure methane emissions simulated with manure- DNDC for the liquid storage tank when compared to the IPCC rec- ommended factors has been recognized in past studies. For instance, VanderZaag et al. (2013) found that the range of tier 1 (cool climate) methane EFs for liquid dairy manure (132-159 g head d-¹ to be much smaller than average summer and spring factors based on emission measurements: 249 g CH4 cow 1 in

the spring and 673 g CH4 cow in the summer (i.e. ranging from 1.9 to 5.1 times higher). Baldé et al. (2016) also found the U.S. EPA's manure methane emissions model that the IPCC tier 2 MCFs are partly based on (Mangino et al., 2001), tend to underestimate GHG emissions by up to 60% of the total annual observed CH4 emissions from a storage tank.

One challenge when applying the IPCC tier 2 MCFs for liquid manure storage is that one needs to choose between a liquid slurry tank and an anaerobic lagoon. In both cases manure decomposes anaerobically, however, the prescribed MCFs for the tank and lagoon systems are substantially lower for the tank system in cool- to-temperate climates. As a result, estimated liquid manure methane emissions can be underestimated for systems that are defined as liquid slurry storage tanks and not earthen lagoons in cases where manure tanks are not completely emptied as this can enable both systems to achieve similar volatile solid to methane conversion rates (Baldé et al., 2016). For instance, the assumption that 4% of slurry remained in the tank after each emptying event was made and this was typically observed at the actual farm. While the liquid manure storage system in this study is a concrete liquid manure storage tank, the estimated MCFs are closer to the tier 2 MCFs for an anaerobic lagoon system. The simulated MFCs found in this analysis are also in agreement with MCFs based on measure- ments for the same farm system-57% in year 1 and 72% in year 2 (Baldé et al., 2016).
21: <header>3.5. Benchmarking </header>

Fig. 5 provides a comparison of the life cycle GHG emissions per kg FPCM (both before and after allocation) between several past Canadian studies and the scenarios in this study using both the tier 3 results and the tier 2 MM CH4 emission factors (see SM: tab "tier2_assumptions" for details). The LCA results from this study are higher than results using the tier 2 CH4 factors along with findings from past studies. The main reason for these differences is due to the assumed GHG emissions from MM (i.e. at dairy farm before field application).
Comparing results before allocation, the GHG emissions gener- ated from MM for the DFB and DFC scenarios (using process

simulations) range from 3.8 to 14 and from 1.7 to 6.1 times higher than these previous studies, respectively. For Arsenault et al. (2009)'s confined farm system, all manure was stored as liquid slurry in a tank and they followed IPCC (1996) tier 2 guidelines which recommends a methane conversion factor (MCF) of 10% (IPCC, 1996). On the other hand, Jayasundara and Wagner-Riddle (2013) apply a tier 2 (IPCC, 2006) weighted average MCF for manure storage where they assumed 40% of manure treatment in the Ontario system was handled as liquids (MCF = 18%), and 43% and 17% of manure was handled as solids (MCF = 2%) or excreted onto pasture (MCF = 1%), respectively. The lower GHG intensity (0.92 kg CO2eq kg FPCM, before allocation) for milk production found by Mc Geough et al. (2012) was mostly explained by the following assumptions: i) the use of a solid manure management system (i.e. MCF = 2%); and ii) CO2eq credits generated from exported manure were used for replacing synthetic fertilizer N in a receiving farm beyond the dairy farm system boundaries. These differences in GHG intensity are observed for similar reasons in a Canada-wide study that estimated the average GHG intensity of raw milk at farm gate to be 1.07 kg CO2eq kg™ milk (inter-pro- vincial averages ranged from 0.86 to 1.14 kg CO2eq kg- milk) (Vergé et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. 5, when the appropriate tier 2 MM CH4 factors are applied in our study (MCF = 17%), then results fall within the range of previous Canadian studies.

For the DFB and DFC systems in this study, the MCFs derived from the average methane rate as determined from the manure- DNDC simulations (tier 3 results) for manure storage in the con- crete tank equalled 66% and 71%, respectively. These estimated MCFs are much higher than the IPPC tier 2 recommendations for concrete storage tanks with natural crust cover (17%) and within the same magnitude as the IPCC tier 2 recommendations for an uncovered anaerobic lagoon with an average annual temperature less than 10° C (MCF = 66%)-the annual average temperature in the region (Ottawa) is about 6° C.

The high manure methane emissions simulated with manure- DNDC for the liquid storage tank when compared to the IPCC rec- ommended factors has been recognized in past studies. For instance, VanderZaag et al. (2013) found that the range of tier 1 (cool climate) methane EFs for liquid dairy manure (132-159 g head d-¹ to be much smaller than average summer and spring factors based on emission measurements: 249 g CH4 cow 1 in

One challenge when applying the IPCC tier 2 MCFs for liquid manure storage is that one needs to choose between a liquid slurry tank and an anaerobic lagoon.
