22: The total annual life-cycle GHG emissions estimated for the DFB and DFC systems are 4.07 and 2.61 kilo-t CO2eq yr 1 (i.e. with no allocation), respectively. For this particular farm system, the miti- gation opportunity from transitioning from the DFB to the DFC system was estimated to be about 1460 t CO2eq yr If jurisdictions recognize this manure composting technology as a viable carbon offset option, investment in this equipment would likely become worthwhile. For instance, as the price on carbon in Canada in- creases from $10 (in 2018) to $50 (in 2022) per t CO2eq (Tombe, 2016), the adoption rate of this technology would likely increase since the carbon credits attributed to the separator-composter from the studied farm would increase from around $ 14,000 yr to over $ 70,000 yr 1 Therefore, it is recommended that jurisdictions recognize manure composter technologies as a viable GHG miti- gation opportunity in their carbon offset programs.
According to a recent Canada-wide survey, Sheppard et al.

(2011) found that 72% of the manure of lactating COWS was handled as slurry and this is the most common handling approach on larger farms and is increasingly prevalent (VanderZaag et al., 2013). Also, of the manure handled as slurry, only 10% of the farms had solids separated from liquids and this was largely done by decanting liquids after settling (Sheppard et al., 2011). These survey results indicate that there is significant potential in Canada for dairy farms to adopt this type of manure liquid-solid separator technology. Also, since the negative trade-offs of adopting this technology are minor, its mitigation potential is likely just as promising in other countries. Therefore, it is recommended that jurisdictions should work to streamline and incentivize the adop- tion process for dairy farmers that meet the correct criteria for implementing separator-composter technologies.
23: The total annual life-cycle GHG emissions estimated for the DFB and DFC systems are 4.07 and 2.61 kilo-t CO2eq yr 1 (i.e. with no allocation), respectively. For this particular farm system, the miti- gation opportunity from transitioning from the DFB to the DFC system was estimated to be about 1460 t CO2eq yr If jurisdictions recognize this manure composting technology as a viable carbon offset option, investment in this equipment would likely become worthwhile. For instance, as the price on carbon in Canada in- creases from $10 (in 2018) to $50 (in 2022) per t CO2eq (Tombe, 2016), the adoption rate of this technology would likely increase since the carbon credits attributed to the separator-composter from the studied farm would increase from around $ 14,000 yr to over $ 70,000 yr 1 Therefore, it is recommended that jurisdictions recognize manure composter technologies as a viable GHG miti- gation opportunity in their carbon offset programs.
According to a recent Canada-wide survey, Sheppard et al.

(2011) found that 72% of the manure of lactating COWS was handled as slurry and this is the most common handling approach on larger farms and is increasingly prevalent (VanderZaag et al., 2013). Also, of the manure handled as slurry, only 10% of the farms had solids separated from liquids and this was largely done by decanting liquids after settling (Sheppard et al., 2011). These survey results indicate that there is significant potential in Canada for dairy farms to adopt this type of manure liquid-solid separator technology. Also, since the negative trade-offs of adopting this technology are minor, its mitigation potential is likely just as promising in other countries. Therefore, it is recommended that jurisdictions should work to streamline and incentivize the adop- tion process for dairy farmers that meet the correct criteria for implementing separator-composter technologies.
24: The primary findings from this paper indicate that from a life- cycle perspective there is a promising climate change mitigation opportunity when manure separator-composters are adopted at farms reliant on large liquid manure storage tanks. This analysis provided insight into the GHG emission and environmental impact trade-offs of adopting an automatic manure separator-composter system. The GHG emission reductions attributed to the composter system far out-weigh key burdens such as the increase in on-farm electricity requirements and the increased methane emissions from the barn floor due to the composted manure bedding. Apart from the significant methane reductions in the liquid manure storage tank (63% less), the composted manure enabled the soil carbon to accumulate at a faster rate than when only organic carbon from the liquid manure storage tank is spread on the local dairy crop complex (-23 versus -53 g CO2eq kg) 1 FPCM).

The GHG emissions modelled with manure-DNDC were cali- brated to representative farm measurements, and thus provide confidence in the overall carbon footprint of the DFB and DFC systems. The methane conversion factors calculated for the liquid storage tank were significantly higher than what the IPCC tier 2 recommendations prescribe for liquid/slurry systems. Given this high disparity, it is recommended that future GHG emissions cal- culations for livestock systems that rely on liquid manure storage adopt tier 3 process based simulation models that are preferably calibrated and validated to regional conditions. This will require greater cooperation between the manure/crop process modeling and LCA modeling communities.

25: The primary findings from this paper indicate that from a life- cycle perspective there is a promising climate change mitigation opportunity when manure separator-composters are adopted at farms reliant on large liquid manure storage tanks. This analysis provided insight into the GHG emission and environmental impact trade-offs of adopting an automatic manure separator-composter system. The GHG emission reductions attributed to the composter system far out-weigh key burdens such as the increase in on-farm electricity requirements and the increased methane emissions from the barn floor due to the composted manure bedding. Apart from the significant methane reductions in the liquid manure storage tank (63% less), the composted manure enabled the soil carbon to accumulate at a faster rate than when only organic carbon from the liquid manure storage tank is spread on the local dairy crop complex (-23 versus -53 g CO2eq kg) 1 FPCM).

The GHG emissions modelled with manure-DNDC were cali- brated to representative farm measurements, and thus provide confidence in the overall carbon footprint of the DFB and DFC systems. The methane conversion factors calculated for the liquid storage tank were significantly higher than what the IPCC tier 2 recommendations prescribe for liquid/slurry systems. Given this high disparity, it is recommended that future GHG emissions cal- culations for livestock systems that rely on liquid manure storage adopt tier 3 process based simulation models that are preferably calibrated and validated to regional conditions. This will require greater cooperation between the manure/crop process modeling and LCA modeling communities.

27: The primary findings from this paper indicate that from a life- cycle perspective there is a promising climate change mitigation opportunity when manure separator-composters are adopted at farms reliant on large liquid manure storage tanks. This analysis provided insight into the GHG emission and environmental impact trade-offs of adopting an automatic manure separator-composter system. The GHG emission reductions attributed to the composter system far out-weigh key burdens such as the increase in on-farm electricity requirements and the increased methane emissions from the barn floor due to the composted manure bedding. Apart from the significant methane reductions in the liquid manure storage tank (63% less), the composted manure enabled the soil carbon to accumulate at a faster rate than when only organic carbon from the liquid manure storage tank is spread on the local dairy crop complex (-23 versus -53 g CO2eq kg) 1 FPCM).

The GHG emissions modelled with manure-DNDC were cali- brated to representative farm measurements, and thus provide confidence in the overall carbon footprint of the DFB and DFC systems. The methane conversion factors calculated for the liquid storage tank were significantly higher than what the IPCC tier 2 recommendations prescribe for liquid/slurry systems. Given this high disparity, it is recommended that future GHG emissions cal- culations for livestock systems that rely on liquid manure storage adopt tier 3 process based simulation models that are preferably calibrated and validated to regional conditions. This will require greater cooperation between the manure/crop process modeling and LCA modeling communities.

30: The primary findings from this paper indicate that from a life- cycle perspective there is a promising climate change mitigation opportunity when manure separator-composters are adopted at farms reliant on large liquid manure storage tanks. This analysis provided insight into the GHG emission and environmental impact trade-offs of adopting an automatic manure separator-composter system. The GHG emission reductions attributed to the composter system far out-weigh key burdens such as the increase in on-farm electricity requirements and the increased methane emissions from the barn floor due to the composted manure bedding. Apart from the significant methane reductions in the liquid manure storage tank (63% less), the composted manure enabled the soil carbon to accumulate at a faster rate than when only organic carbon from the liquid manure storage tank is spread on the local dairy crop complex (-23 versus -53 g CO2eq kg) 1 FPCM).

The GHG emissions modelled with manure-DNDC were cali- brated to representative farm measurements, and thus provide confidence in the overall carbon footprint of the DFB and DFC systems. The methane conversion factors calculated for the liquid storage tank were significantly higher than what the IPCC tier 2 recommendations prescribe for liquid/slurry systems. Given this high disparity, it is recommended that future GHG emissions cal- culations for livestock systems that rely on liquid manure storage adopt tier 3 process based simulation models that are preferably calibrated and validated to regional conditions. This will require greater cooperation between the manure/crop process modeling and LCA modeling communities.

32: The primary findings from this paper indicate that from a life- cycle perspective there is a promising climate change mitigation opportunity when manure separator-composters are adopted at farms reliant on large liquid manure storage tanks. This analysis provided insight into the GHG emission and environmental impact trade-offs of adopting an automatic manure separator-composter system. The GHG emission reductions attributed to the composter system far out-weigh key burdens such as the increase in on-farm electricity requirements and the increased methane emissions from the barn floor due to the composted manure bedding. Apart from the significant methane reductions in the liquid manure storage tank (63% less), the composted manure enabled the soil carbon to accumulate at a faster rate than when only organic carbon from the liquid manure storage tank is spread on the local dairy crop complex (-23 versus -53 g CO2eq kg) 1 FPCM).

The GHG emissions modelled with manure-DNDC were cali- brated to representative farm measurements, and thus provide confidence in the overall carbon footprint of the DFB and DFC systems. The methane conversion factors calculated for the liquid storage tank were significantly higher than what the IPCC tier 2 recommendations prescribe for liquid/slurry systems. Given this high disparity, it is recommended that future GHG emissions cal- culations for livestock systems that rely on liquid manure storage adopt tier 3 process based simulation models that are preferably calibrated and validated to regional conditions. This will require greater cooperation between the manure/crop process modeling and LCA modeling communities.
